title: Lost in the Middle
concept: Research shows LLMs are excellent at recalling information at the start or end of a long prompt, but struggle to find details buried in the middle.

exercises.prompt: What happens when you give an LLM a very long prompt?

options.text: They struggle to recall details in the middle of long prompts.
They can only read the first 10 words.

exercises.explain: LLMs pay attention differently depending on where information is located. They struggle most with details in the middle of long texts.

cards.title: The AI Attention Gap
cards.body: Have you ever read a giant textbook chapter and immediately forgot everything in the middle pages? Believe it or not, super-smart AI models have the exact same problem!

cards.title: What is 'Lost in the Middle'?
cards.body: 'Lost in the Middle' is a phenomenon where Large Language Models (LLMs) struggle to recall or use information that is placed in the middle of a long input prompt, while easily remembering info at the start or end.

cards.title: The Grocery List Effect
cards.body: Think of a long grocery list. You easily remember the first item (milk) and the last item (apples). But you completely forget the cereal buried right in the middle! LLMs do the exact same thing when reading long texts.

cards.title: A Missing Fact
cards.body: If you paste a long history article and ask a question, the LLM will answer accurately if the clue is in the first paragraph. If the clue is in paragraph 15 of 30, the LLM is much more likely to hallucinate or say it doesn't know.

cards.title: Myth: Big Context Means Perfect Memory
cards.body: Many people think that if an LLM has a 'huge context window', it will remember every single word perfectly. In reality, size doesn't guarantee attention—the middle still gets ignored!

cards.title: The Attention Curve
cards.body: LLMs pay the most attention to the start and end of your prompts. Information in the middle gets buried.

left.text: Information placed at the very beginning or the very end of your prompt.

right.text: Information buried deep within the middle paragraphs of a long document.

exercises.prompt: In a very long prompt, where is an LLM most likely to successfully find and recall information?

options.text: At the exact midpoint of the text
Spread out randomly across the whole text
At the very beginning and the very end
Only in the footnotes and author credits

exercises.explain: Research shows that LLMs have a U-shaped attention curve, meaning their retrieval performance is highest at the beginning and the end of the input.

exercises.prompt: True or False: If an LLM has a large context window, it is guaranteed to recall information in the middle just as well as at the beginning.
exercises.explain: Even with massive context limits (like 100k tokens), LLMs still suffer from decreased accuracy for information located in the middle of the prompt.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe where LLMs struggle to recall information.
exercises.sentence: AI models often lose information that is placed in the ______ of a long prompt.
exercises.explain: LLMs are great at reading the start and end of prompts, but they frequently lose track of details hidden right in the middle.

exercises.prompt: Which of these outputs demonstrates the 'Lost in the Middle' problem?

options.text: An LLM ignores a key rule that was placed in paragraph 5 of a 10-paragraph prompt.
An LLM perfectly summarizes the entire document from start to finish.
An LLM refuses to answer because the prompt contains inappropriate language.

exercises.explain: Option A shows the 'Lost in the Middle' effect, where the AI misses a key detail because it was buried in the center of a massive block of text.

exercises.prompt: You are building a customer support bot that must follow a strict safety rule. Where should you place this rule in your prompt to ensure the LLM doesn't forget it?

options.text: Hide the instruction in the exact middle of a 10-page document.
Put the critical instruction at the very beginning of the prompt.
Repeat the instruction ten times only in the middle paragraph.
Make the prompt as long as possible without any structure.

exercises.explain: To help an LLM find a critical detail, place it at the very beginning or the very end of your prompt, rather than burying it in the middle.
